events.event: The Acts of Union come into effect, joining the Kingdoms of England and Scotland into Great Britain.
The United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland is established as the Act of Union 1800 takes effect.
The British Broadcasting Corporation is established by Royal Charter.
The discovery of the planet Vulcan is announced at the French Academy of Sciences; British astronomers later spend years debating its existence.
The birth of Michael Tippett, the renowned British composer of 'A Child of Our Time'.
The Ibrox Disaster occurs in Glasgow, leading to a major safety review in British football stadiums.
The birth of J.R.R. Tolkien, the author of 'The Lord of the Rings' and Oxford professor.
British explorer Howard Carter discovers the sarcophagus of Tutankhamun in the Valley of the Kings.
Admiral Sir Bertram Ramsay, architect of the Dunkirk evacuation, dies in a plane crash.
The birth of Sir Isaac Newton in Lincolnshire, a foundational figure in physics and mathematics.
Donald Campbell dies on Coniston Water whilst attempting the world water speed record in Bluebird K7.
The final episode of 'Top of the Pops' is recorded before its cancellation as a weekly programme.
The death of Edward the Confessor, which leads to the succession crisis and the Norman Conquest.
The birth of Edward Bond, the influential and often controversial British playwright.
The first ever One Day International cricket match is played between Australia and England.
Harold Godwinson is crowned King of England following the death of Edward the Confessor.
King Henry VIII marries his fourth wife, Anne of Cleves.
The birth of Loretta Young; the Hollywood actress was a popular figure for British cinema audiences.
France retakes Calais, the last continental possession of the Kingdom of England.
The birth of George Simon, Earl of Athlone, a member of the British Royal Family and Governor General of Canada.
The Leaning Tower of Pisa is closed to the public for safety; the news is a major topic in the British press.
The birth of John Curtin, the Australian Prime Minister who led during WWII and maintained close ties to the UK.
The birth of Stephen Hawking in Oxford, one of the most brilliant theoretical physicists of the modern era.
The birth of David Bowie in Brixton, a transformative figure in British music and culture.
British Prime Minister William Pitt the Younger introduces income tax to help fund the Napoleonic Wars.
The funeral of Lord Nelson takes place at St Paul's Cathedral following his victory at Trafalgar.
The birth of Richard Nixon; the President's interactions with UK leaders remain a point of historical study.
The Penny Post is introduced in the UK, revolutionising communication by making mail affordable.
The Metropolitan Railway, the world's first underground railway, opens between Paddington and Farringdon.
The first General Assembly of the United Nations opens in London.
The death of Sir Hans Sloane, whose vast collection founded the British Museum.
The Open University awards its first degrees, marking a milestone in British higher education.
The birth of Bryan Robson, the legendary England and Manchester United footballer.
Elizabeth I travels from the Tower of London to Westminster Abbey for her coronation.
Motown Records is founded; its music would go on to have a profound influence on British pop culture.
The death of Dame Agatha Christie, the world-renowned British crime novelist.
The death of George Fox, the founder of the Religious Society of Friends (Quakers) in England.
The Independent Labour Party holds its first meeting in Bradford, chaired by Keir Hardie.
The first episode of 'The David Frost Show' airs, becoming a staple of British television journalism.
The death of Lewis Carroll, author of 'Alice's Adventures in Wonderland' and Oxford academic.
The birth of Mark Oliphant, the Australian-British physicist who played a key role in the development of radar.
The birth of Jim Duggan; while American, his wrestling career was widely broadcast on British TV screens.
Elizabeth I is crowned Queen of England in Westminster Abbey.
The British Museum opens its doors to the public for the first time.
The birth of James Nesbitt, the celebrated Northern Irish actor.
Ivan the Terrible is crowned Tsar of Russia; Anglo-Russian trade relations begin to develop shortly after.
The birth of Wim Duisenberg; the economist's work on the Euro had significant political implications for the UK.
The birth of Bobby Zamora, the former England and West Ham United striker.
The birth of Benjamin Franklin, who spent many years in London as a diplomat for the American colonies.
Captain James Cook becomes the first European to cross the Antarctic Circle.
The birth of Steve Earle; the musician's folk and country influence is widely felt in the UK music scene.
The birth of A.A. Milne, the author of the 'Winnie-the-Pooh' books.
The birth of Peter Smithson, the influential British architect behind New Brutalism.
The birth of Pep Guardiola; the manager's impact on British football through Manchester City is monumental.
The birth of James Watt, the Scottish engineer whose steam engine improvements drove the Industrial Revolution.
The birth of Sir Henry Bessemer, the engineer who developed the first process for mass-producing steel.
The birth of Jenson Button, the 2009 Formula One World Champion from Somerset.
Simon de Montfort summons the first parliament to include commoners as well as nobles.
The death of King George V and the accession of Edward VIII.
The birth of Gary Barlow, the singer-songwriter and member of the British group Take That.
The birth of John Sheffield, 1st Duke of Buckingham and Normanby, a prominent Tory politician and poet.
The birth of Benny Hill, the comedian whose variety show was a staple of British TV for decades.
The birth of Nicky Butt, the decorated England and Manchester United midfielder.
The birth of Francis Bacon, the English philosopher and statesman who developed the scientific method.
The Battle of Rorke's Drift begins, a famous engagement during the Anglo-Zulu War.
The death of Queen Victoria after a reign of nearly 64 years.
The Royal Exchange in London is opened by Queen Elizabeth I.
The death of William Pitt the Younger, the youngest person to serve as British Prime Minister.
The birth of Bob Paisley, the most successful manager in the history of Liverpool Football Club.

The Boteler Legacy: The will of Richard Boteler of Biddenham is proved. This marks an early recorded chapter for the family who would become the village's most prominent landowners, eventually building the Manor and the famous dovecote.
Franklin D. Roosevelt and Winston Churchill conclude the Casablanca Conference during WWII.
The death of Sir Winston Churchill, Britain's wartime Prime Minister.
The 'Wapping Dispute' begins as Rupert Murdoch moves production of his newspapers to a new high-tech plant.
Henry VIII secretly marries Anne Boleyn, his second wife.
The birth of Robert Burns, the celebrated national poet of Scotland.
The 'Gang of Four' issues the Limehouse Declaration, leading to the formation of the Social Democratic Party.
The First Fleet arrives at Sydney Cove, led by British Admiral Arthur Phillip.
The death of General Charles George Gordon during the Siege of Khartoum.
The birth of Andrew Ridgeley, member of the hugely successful British pop duo Wham!.
The birth of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart; he performed in London as a child prodigy in 1764.
The birth of Lewis Carroll (Charles Lutwidge Dodgson) in Cheshire.
The birth of Rosamund Pike, the internationally acclaimed British actress.
The death of King Henry VIII and the accession of his son, Edward VI.
Jane Austen's 'Pride and Prejudice' is published anonymously in London.
The birth of Jamie Carragher, the long-serving Liverpool and England defender.
Queen Victoria institutes the Victoria Cross, the highest award for bravery in the British armed forces.
The birth of Sydney Chapman, the British geophysicist who made major contributions to the study of the ozone layer.
The first broadcast of 'Desert Island Discs' on the BBC Home Service.
King Charles I is executed outside the Banqueting House in Whitehall.
The state funeral of Sir Winston Churchill is held at St Paul's Cathedral.
The birth of Olivia Colman, the Academy Award-winning British actress.
Guy Fawkes is executed for his part in the Gunpowder Plot.
The death of Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie), the Jacobite pretender to the British throne.
The launch of 'Breakfast Time' on BBC One, the UK's first regular breakfast television programme.
